data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Strong Execution, Stretched Valuation
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks, Inc.'s (PANW) business has been surprisingly resilient in 2025, supported by its XSIAM, SASE and software firewall products. This situation is likely to persist going forward due to AI demand, the nascent state of the SASE market and the emerging legacy SIEM replacement opportunity.

Palo Alto's planned CyberArk (CYBR) acquisition is also a potentially important development. I have written about CyberArk in the past, and believe it is the best positioned company in the identity security space. The importance of identity protection is likely to increase in coming years with the proliferation of agents, which is highlighted by CrowdStrike's (CRWD) recent decision to acquire SGNL.

I previously suggested that Palo Alto's stock was likely to offer limited upside in the near-term. The stock is down around 5% since then, with returns in 2025 particularly weak in comparison to the broader market. Palo Alto's distribution capabilities and comprehensive product portfolio should ensure it continues to have success. Valuation remains a concern, though, and likely means that returns will be closer to the market than the strong performance achieved between 2017 and 2024.

Market Conditions

AI is contributing to a resurgence of cloud transformation projects at the moment, supporting network security demand. Data security is also a hot area at the moment, due in large part to security concerns created by LLMs. While hardware is becoming a less important part of Palo Alto's business, appliance demand is also stable.

Palo Alto now estimates that it has a $200 billion TAM, which is expected to grow to $300 billion by 2028, supported by the additions of Chronosphere and CyberArk. This is largely made up of firewall, SASE, SOC and cloud security, which are all areas of strength for Palo Alto.

Palo Alto Business Updates

Like many companies in the cybersecurity space, much of Palo Alto's current focus is on automation, data and adapting to the changes brought by AI. This includes the recent acquisition of Chronosphere for approximately $3.35 billion. Chronosphere is an observability service provider with a scalable architecture that provides a cost advantage compared to other solutions on the market (~1/3 of the cost of other leading solutions). Chronosphere also recently acquired Calyptia, a data pipeline provider. This expands Palo Alto's business into areas like observability, security data pipelines and telemetry optimization. It also provides Palo Alto with broader observability capabilities than peers like CrowdStrike. While the price tag is steep, Chronosphere has around $160 million ARR and a triple-digit growth rate. Observability constitutes around 10-15% of infrastructure spend, and as a result, Palo Alto believes this could be an opportunity worth tens of billions of dollars. While current leaders include AppDynamics, Dynatrace and Datadog, Chronosphere is used by some of the large LLM providers.

The Chronosphere acquisition will also help Palo Alto to capitalize on the legacy SIEM replacement opportunity. Solutions from the likes of ArcSight, LogRhythm, QRadar and Splunk are outdated (lack of scalability, high costs, labor intensive) and are now in the process of being replaced. Palo Alto has roughly 470 XSIAM customers, and the average ARR per customer is over $1 million. This compares to 270 XSIAM customers in Q3 FY25 and 400 XSIAM customers in the fourth quarter. While this suggests a recent deceleration in the pace of customer acquisition, this is likely due to the easing of QRadar acquisition tailwinds.

Palo Alto has also acquired Protect AI, helping it to provide protection across the AI lifecycle. The acquisition forms the cornerstone of Prisma AIRS, Palo Alto's AI security platform.

In addition, Palo Alto recently launched Cortex AgentiX, which is the next generation of Cortex XSOAR. AgentiX provides a platform to build, deploy and govern agents for security and IT operations. Agents are an area where identity security is critical, and the adoption of GenAI and agents likely provided the impetus for Palo Alto's planned acquisition of CyberArk, a Privileged Access Management vendor, for approximately $25 billion (combination of cash and equity). While CyberArk had traded on rich multiples in recent years, it has a strong business and appears to be in the right place at the right time. AI is likely to increase the importance of identity, and Palo Alto has suggested that IAM doesn't provide the necessary security.

Palo Alto's SASE ARR increased 34% YoY in the first quarter, surpassing $1.3 billion. The company now has 6,800 SASE customers, including one third of the Fortune 500. While SASE strength appears broad-based, Palo Alto's secure browser is a growth driver. The company has around 1,200 secure browser customers and a cumulative total of more than 7.5 million browsers had been sold by the end of the first quarter. This is strategically important, as the majority of most white-collar employees work is performed in the browser.

While the businesses falling under Palo Alto's NGS banner are maturing, leading to more moderate growth, there is still a long growth runway. With the planned acquisitions, Palo Alto is now targeting $20 billion NGS ARR by FY2030. This is an optimistic target, though, which will require growth stabilizing in the mid 20% range. Growth continues to be driven by larger customers, with Palo Alto now having 169 customers with more than $5 million NGS ARR, up 54% YoY, and 55 customers with more than $10 million NGS ARR, up 49% YoY.

Quantum computing is also now being touted as a potential area of growth, with Palo Alto suggesting that its quantum security TAM is around $10 billion. This is based on a belief that quantum computing will be commercialized by 2029 and that companies need to start preparing now. In support of this, Palo Alto has introduced solutions to help companies take inventory of cryptographic risk and make legacy systems quantum safe. The company's Gen 5 firewalls are also optimized for quantum security. Palo Alto is also partnering with IBM to deliver solutions for post-quantum cryptography.

Financial Analysis

Palo Alto generated $2.47 billion of revenue in the first quarter, an increase of 16% YoY. RPO increased 24% YoY in the first quarter, although current RPO was only up 16%. NGS ARR totaled $5.85 billion, up 29% YoY. Product revenue grew 23% YoY, with around half of product revenue coming from software form factors. This part of the business is currently being supported by AI related demand. There remains a large opportunity here given that Palo Alto only has around 12,500 software firewall customers versus a total of 80,000 firewall customers.

NGS ARR growth in the second quarter is expected to be 28%, while full year NGS ARR growth is expected to be in the 26-27% range. Second quarter revenue is expected to be between $2.57 billion and $2.59 billion, an increase of 14% to 15%. For the full year, revenue is expected to be $10.50-$10.54 billion, up 14% YoY. While this suggests fairly stable growth through the year, the CyberArk acquisition is expected to close in fiscal Q3 and the Chronosphere acquisition is expected to close in the second half of the year.

Palo Alto's margins also continue to improve as its business matures. While the company's free cash flow margin is already high, this isn't really a good indicator of the underlying profitability of the business. Palo Alto has strong unit economics though, and its GAAP operating margin is likely to approach 30% at maturity. This is a positive, but it is already largely reflected in Palo Alto's revenue multiple, which is high for a company with a mid-teens growth rate.

Conclusion

Analysts expect Palo Alto to maintain a low double-digit revenue growth rate going forward. Identity, SIEM and observability are expected to be growth drivers going forward, and there remains a large SASE opportunity which Palo Alto is well positioned to capitalize on.

Including the CyberArk and Chronosphere acquisitions, Palo Alto is now targeting $20 billion NGS ARR by FY2030. This is a fairly aggressive target, though, which will require ARR growth to begin stabilizing in the mid 20% range. It would also probably only be sufficient for mid to low teen returns due to Palo Alto's relatively high revenue multiple.

While Palo Alto has a strong business that continues to perform well, there are reasons to be cautious beyond just valuation. XSIAM customer growth has stalled as Palo Alto has moved past the boost provided by QRadar. SASE customer growth is also moderating, which will contribute to an ongoing NGS growth slowdown, and platformizations decelerated sharply in the first quarter.

As a result, I believe that a number of cybersecurity companies are more attractive opportunities than Palo Alto at this point in time. This is supported by the fact that Palo Alto did not repurchase any shares in Q1, after stating its buyback strategy is opportunistic. Palo Alto's CTO has also sold a large number of shares in recent months, although this may not be that informative about the company's prospects.

data.title: Amazon: The Ultimate Nexus Of Growth Catalysts
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) is significantly undervalued, as its positive earnings revisions significantly outpaced the modest 10% share price growth over the last twelve months. Despite underperformance compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) on a twelve-months horizon, the stock is gaining its momentum as it has significantly outperformed since October 26, when I shared my previous call. There were various positive recent developments around the company's most important businesses, the e-commerce and cloud infrastructure. Moreover, the forward P/E ratio setup is extremely bullish while Wall Street analysts believe AMZN is undervalued by 20%. AMZN remains a Strong Buy.

Fundamental analysis

Let's start with briefly summarizing Amazon's latest earnings release (Q3) since it was published after my previous call. Amazon's Q3 revenue grew by 13.4% on a year-over-year basis, which is impressive growth given that the last three years' revenue CAGR was 11.2%. From the EPS perspective standpoint, there was not only an impressive 36% year-over-year growth, but also a strong 25% positive surprise. Finally, the company demonstrated revenue strength across all businesses, as each segment delivered double-digit top line growth in Q3.

If we speak about the core business, e-commerce, Amazon remains the world's leader in this industry. It is a mature business, where future EPS growth mostly depends on the macro environment and cost-efficiency. From the macro environment perspective, the situation looks favorable for Amazon. The U.S. economy's growth in Q3, the latest available quarter, surprised economists with a 4.3% GDP growth compared to forecasted 3.2%. Furthermore, Fitch Ratings recently raised their GDP growth forecast for the U.S. for both 2025 and 2026.

There are other positive signs from the macro environment which are likely to be tailwinds for Amazon's e-commerce business. The country's labor market is still strong despite recent uptick in the unemployment rate, as it is still notably below long-term historical averages. Finally, despite all the fears of Spring 2025 regarding new tariffs' effect on inflation, the Fed does not plan to tighten monetary policy in 2026. With all that being said, the broader environment looks favorable for this business' revenue growth potential.

From the costs side perspective, key managers of this business are also working on streamlining operations. For example, the company strengthens partnerships with e-commerce logistics providers, like the one with Aeromexico Cargo. Besides partnerships, there is a crucial trend suggesting that despite aggressive growth of the company's e-commerce business after the pandemic, the headcount stayed approximately flat while the number of robots has almost doubled between 2022 and 2025. I see great potential to generate EPS gains from increasing the level of automatization, which will highly likely let the company to begin steadily cutting its 1.5 million headcount in the foreseeable future.

Amazon's second largest business, AWS, still leads in the global cloud infrastructure market with a 29% market share. Amazon's market share in the cloud market is larger than Microsoft's (MSFT) by nine percentage points, which is a solid gap. As the industry's undisputed leader, Amazon is extremely well positioned to capitalize on the projected 12.3% CAGR for the industry over the next decade. This industry is currently supercharged by AI tailwinds, where Amazon also succeeds.

Besides Amazon's leading position in cloud infrastructure industry, the company also boasts a solid network of partnerships to push AI innovation. Within just a few past days, Amazon inked a couple remarkable partnerships in the AI domain. The one with Infosys, aims to drive enterprise generative AI adoption via integration of Amazon's Q Developer with Infosys Topaz. It is extremely promising as the combined solution is designed to enhance processes spanning various use cases such as software development, HR, sales, automatic documentation, and vendor management.

Another interesting partnership inked by AWS recently is the one with Aumovio, a German technology and electronics company involved in development of autonomous vehicles. This partnership means that Amazon's cloud solutions are advanced and reliable enough to handle data processing for safety validation and real-world AI. The intersection of AI and automotive industries is one of the most promising mega-trends of the modern era as the autonomous vehicle market is expected to deliver a 35% CAGR over the next decade.

Besides direct involvement of AWS in AI-related projects, let's not forget that Amazon is one of the major investors in Anthropic, one of the hottest private generative AI companies in the world. This generative AI startup reportedly plans to raise $10 billion at $350 valuation. The previous round took place in early September when Anthropic was valued at $183 billion. Almost a 2x increase in Anthropic's (ANTHRO) valuation means Amazon will record hefty capital gains from this investment in 2026.

To summarize, with so many positive developments around the company's two major businesses, I think that maintaining bullish outlook is reasonable. Moreover, Amazon appears substantially undervalued at the moment.

Valuation analysis

Amazon's forward P/E setup is extremely bullish as the multiple is expected to almost half by FY2028. I believe that this forecast is reasonable because developments around Amazon's fundamentals demonstrate that future EPS growth will be supported by both revenue growth and keeping costs under control. Moreover, Amazon has stellar EPS growth record with a staggering 70% CAGR over the last decade. Amazon's estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio of 19 looks extremely attractive considering the company's leadership in both cloud infrastructure and e-commerce, both industries expected to demonstrate double-digit CAGR over the next several years.

My assertion that AMZN is very attractively valued is also confirmed by Wall Street analysts. The average target price for AMZN is $296, which means a 20% upside from AMZN's current share price. This upside alone is already compelling for a stock like AMZN. However, I think there are great chances that this target price might see upward revisions. Wall Street's consensus price directly depends on EPS revisions, and Amazon's stellar fundamentals increase chances for new EPS forecast upgrades.

Moreover, there is strong momentum in EPS revisions. This can be seen from the above schedule with Amazon's all positive EPS revisions for several years ahead over the last six months. With rock-solid fundamentals and robust earnings revisions momentum, I think that we are likely to see even more aggressive P/E ratio compression trajectory.

Mitigating factors

While share prices tend to follow fundamentals over the long-term, there might be sentiment-driven short-term share price declines. AMZN gained approximately 11% over the last twelve months, significantly underperforming compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and many other tech stocks with AI exposure. AMZN's relatively weak share price momentum might be the reason why investors would prefer to ignore this stock for some time. It might take a few quarters with massive earnings beats to regain investors' confidence. Therefore, my bullish thesis is for long-term investors. People who want to try to speculate or time the market might get disappointed at the end of the day.

Despite all apparent fundamental positives and attractive valuation, insiders are not rushing to buy the stock. The fact that insiders have been only selling AMZN over the last several months might become another notable share price headwind if they keep selling aggressively. For example, the public might consider continued insider selling as indication that there is limited upside.

Conclusion

AMZN looks extremely attractively valued at the moment, and I tend to agree with the double-digit upside projected by Wall Street analysts. I am highly confident because the management is driving EPS growth not only by pushing revenue expansion but by keeping costs under strict control. With such a comprehensive approach, I believe AMZN will demonstrate solid EPS growth and revisions over the next several quarters.

data.title: Micron: America's Fortress In The Global AI Memory War
data.description:
Investment Thesis

AI supercycle and digital infrastructure expansion are driving demand for memory from technology companies. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) has a unique position as a U.S. manufacturer capable of offering U.S. data center operators the solutions needed without relying on imports. With demand increasing and Micron's capital expenditures including the construction of new factories, the strategic growth is still ahead.

This article will examine not only the catalysts for the company's growth and its financial profile, as well as the competition it faces with the emergence of new players from China. I believe that MU shares still have potential, so we can expect to see continued growth in their price in the short and long term.

Micron's Strategy And Growth Drivers

Micron Technology is a one-of-a-kind company and a leader in semiconductor manufacturing, focusing on solutions for the memory and data storage segment. MU products are the "brains" for processing and storing information in modern AI infrastructure architecture. The company operates in three segments: DRAM (temporary storage of data required for processing requests on digital devices), NAND (long-term data storage in various memory cards, drives, and flash drives), and NOR Flash (providing the fast memory needed to store firmware and software in systems and equipment). Every connected digital device, every request, and every piece of information processed requires data storage, and that is what Micron's technologies are all about. The company is therefore closely linked to the AI boom, with orders for its products directly linked to demand for AI services. Its products are currently used in various business units of its customers, from mobile devices and data storage to embedded IoT systems and data center computing. Given that memory growth is accelerating due to the expansion of AI infrastructure, these strategic developments will be supported by market conditions.

Micron's role will continue to grow in 2026 because it makes superfast HBM3E and HBM4 memory, just what's needed for AI accelerators (for example, those with universal chips from Nvidia (NVDA)). Also, it's an American company because its main factories are in the U.S. On January 16, 2026, it plans to officially start building a new mega-factory in New York State. In total, their production scaling program includes investments of up to $200 billion, allowing them to build two factories in Idaho and four in New York. In addition, the existing plant in Virginia will be modernized. These production plans are aimed at achieving a 40% share of total memory consumption in the U.S. economy.

Considering the current geopolitical agenda, with tensions between the U.S., China, and Russia on the rise again, there could be a bunch of new trade protectionist measures. Due to the fact that many manufacturers are located abroad, in order to ensure the competitiveness of the technology sector and AI infrastructure of the U.S. economy, there is a need for a local manufacturer capable of producing memory solutions on a large scale. Doing so would protect the U.S. from losing access to important technologies possessed by MU.

Competition In The Memory Market

Like many AI-related industries, memory is a highly consolidated market. There are essentially 3 major players in the global market: two South Korean companies, Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK hynix (HXSC.F), and Micron. The undisputed leader is Samsung Electronics because it has the largest production facilities, enabling it to operate in all areas ((DRAM, NAND, and SSD)). SK hynix is battling Micron for the position of second player, as it is the leader in the High Bandwidth Memory, or HBM, sector. Their technology is in high demand from AI accelerators.

But there are other players in the NAND segment that make the competition less intense. This includes the Japanese company Kioxia (specializing exclusively in this segment), the American manufacturer Western Digital (cooperating with Kioxia in the form of joint factories and offering solutions for the SSD market), and SK Hynix's subsidiary Solidigm. The latter is a former Intel division that specializes in the production of enterprise SSDs used in data centers.

As the main trend for Micron in 2026 is HBM3E and HBM4 memory, the company's competition is with Samsung and SK hynix. Here, though, Samsung isn't the leader, but SK hynix, since they've got a key partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) that lets them supply most of the tech. The unique advantage Micron has over its South Korean competitors is its focus on energy efficiency. Its HBM3E consumes 30% less energy than products from other manufacturers. Given the eternal dilemma of power shortages for data centers, a competitive advantage like this could be decisive for customers looking to scale their AI infrastructure.

My analysis of competition in the memory market would be incomplete if I did not mention the new players from China. They will most likely develop their own unique strategy and focus on implementing a program to replace critical technologies in the memory segment for Chinese industry with domestic alternatives. My view is that we'll keep seeing a trend where US and South Korean manufacturers lose market share in the Chinese domestic market. There are two successful projects in China: YMTC and CXMT. Both of these players specialize in their chosen segment. The YMTC is working on the creation of multi-layer NAND memory. According to Chinese customers, these solutions are already successful. And the potential for scaling up production will make it possible to meet growing demand. The CXMT specializes in DRAM memory. Its solutions are still technologically behind, however, due to fairly rapid development, they have the potential to offer competitive and modern solutions to the market within the next 1–2 years. Its main advantage so far is the rapid scaling of production volumes (an expected 68% by 2025), with plans to capture a global share of around 15% of the DRAM market.

Micron's Financial Profile

Micron is one of the few AI infrastructure technology companies that has no issues with its financial profile. Like the positive momentum and the fact that its actual quarterly results are higher than analysts' consensus forecasts (last report showed a positive surprise of 20.71%). And for the next report, EPS growth of 414.27% is forecast compared to the same period a year earlier. Over the past 90 days, 25 revisions to EPS and revenue forecasts have been made, with 100% of the revisions aimed at raising analysts' estimates.

In fiscal Q1 2026, revenue increased 21% to $13.6 billion compared to Q4 2025 and 57% compared to Q1 2025. DRAM sales revenue increased by 20% (versus Q4 2025) to $10.8 billion, whereas NAND sales revenue increased by 22% to $2.7 billion. At the same time, free cash flow once again reached record levels, surpassing forecasts by more than 20%. As a result, forecasts have been raised. In particular, growth in demand for DRAM and NAND has been increased from 10% to 20%. Capital expenditures for 2026 will be $20 billion instead of the previously forecast $18 billion. These changes are driven by the need to scale production more quickly to meet growing demand.

Risks Of Micron

As my recommendation for Micron is "Buy," risks associated with this investment idea are directly related to threat factors that could affect its performance. During the company's conference call following the presentation of its quarterly report for Q1 2026, the management highlighted the risks associated with the inability to meet all demand from current customers, continued restrictions on DRAM and NAND supplies in the coming 2026, the uncertainty surrounding the impact of new tariffs on future results, and potential delays in the construction of clean rooms.

I think the main risk for investing in MU shares is how long the AI boom supercycle will last. Investors today have high expectations for Micron, reflected in its P/E ratio of 31.03x (although the median is 33.38x). Therefore, it appears that the price is based on an ideal scenario. Considering the current policies of the Trump administration and expectations of further interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, it is highly likely that the supercycle will continue. Nevertheless, even the slightest deviation from the aforementioned forecasts could lead to a significant technical correction in the share price. In Q4, 2025, the market was already showing signs of nervousness, as reflected in increased volatility in AI growth stocks. Without any significant threats, I would consider any drop in MU shares of more than 10% to be an ideal opportunity to add to the portfolio.

Conclusion

As a result, Micron's fundamental analysis shows that its shares are highly attractive for investment. AI supercycle will be the main driver of MU's continued share growth. The demand for memory is increasing, as is the company's ability to scale its operations. Large-scale infrastructure projects to build new factories in the U.S. worth up to $200 billion represent a new level of growth. In my opinion, the strategic potential of Micron remains unrealized, although the current share price already reflects the "ideal scenario." All these large-scale projects, combined with increased production volumes, could generate tens of times more revenue and profit in the future. Given geopolitical factors and the need for the U.S. AI sector to be technologically independent from imports, Micron's proposal is more relevant than ever.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amazon: The Ultimate Operating Leverage Story
data.description:
Introduction

Shares of e-commerce player and cloud computing provider Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) have left investors asking for more after returning just 3% in 2025 despite the S&P 500 index (SPX) appreciating in the double-digits. This development is not thanks to fundamental weakness, however, as revenues and net income have both grown substantially. Rather, it is the market not fully appreciating Amazon's long-term earnings potential, which I will have a go at uncovering today.

In my view, Amazon is a Strong Buy and a potential top candidate for 2026 thanks to its:

  • Solid (or even accelerating) revenue growth among its core business lines;
  • AI investments expected to drive additional value and save costs alongside robotics;
  • Margin expansion potential even amid significant long-term investments and AI spend; and
  • Valuation not giving enough to its true earnings power.

AI winner

First, Amazon is a company compounding revenue in the 10-15% region year after year thanks to great execution. This sets a solid baseline for great long-term returns. As seen below, profits, on the other hand, are much more volatile, jumping from $11B to $21B in just a year (2019 to 2020) or dropping from $33B to below zero (2021 to 2022), then to $30B (2023), and then almost doubling YoY (2024). This is why forecasting Amazon's earnings can be quite challenging.

In my view, this portrays their operating leverage, which is greatly enhanced thanks to AI. Amazon has the potential to be a massive AI winner. First, they integrated their AI-powered shopping chatbot Rufus, which can answer questions, give recommendations, sum up reviews, and even store knowledge to propose other products in the future. This removes a lot of friction from the online shopping process and can lead to better customer outcomes, leading to higher revenue growth in the E-Commerce segment in the long run. Rufus has already crossed over 250M users and "could pull in an extra $10B in sales."

Amazon's Cloud segment, AWS, is continuing to grow quickly, and has recently reaccelerated top line growth to 20%, which, being the largest cloud computing provider, is very bullish and points to both great execution and being positioned in a quickly growing market in which demand seems yet to show signs of fatigue. Total commitments even grew 22% YoY, indicating that the revenue acceleration might not just be material for one quarter but more sustainable. Cloud is one of the high-margin segments of AMZN, boasting roughly 35% operating margins, which in my view, could even be slightly increased over the coming years thanks to scalability effects. As a higher percentage of Amazon's total revenue mix comes from AWS, margins should tick higher. Today, AWS already makes up 17.5% of sales.

The same is true for advertising, which is consistently growing above 20% and also of very high-margin nature. In my view, AI will be able to enhance Amazon's advertising for sellers, giving them better placements, better targeting and therefore warranting higher prices, further accelerating growth.

Lastly, Amazon's core e-commerce operation that drive the largest share of revenue leave tons of room to be optimized by robotics. Already today, Amazon employs 750,000 robots in its fulfillment centers and in delivery. These will help improve sorting, packaging and distribution efficiency, reduce labor costs. AI analytics will help reduce wrong shipments, sorting issues, and improve inventory placement. Altogether, more efficient operations have the potential to significantly lower costs while upholding day-to-day business, again boosting margins. Just for 2026, JPMorgan expects $4B in savings from robotics, despite amazon still being in an experimental phase with them. This makes me very optimistic for much higher savings in the future.

At the same time, operating margins are in an uptrend for Amazon despite being in an investment cycle, which goes to show how much more margin potential they have when investments normalize. In my view, this is shown in older investments already paying off today with record operating margins. As such, and as outlined above, I believe Amazon's investments today will also pay off in the future. Considering their high historical ROI rates, this seems likely. So even if Amazon rigorously continues to spend large amounts on R&D, like the $102B on a TTM basis, some margin expansion would STILL be on the table. If they just slightly tone it down, however, they could rapidly increase their profits, showing incredible amounts of operating leverage. Since I trust Amazon's management, I believe it to be bullish for them to invest capital today instead of letting everything pour down to the bottom line.

Valuation

Since AMZN's free cash flow is currently depressed by heavy CapEx, performing a DCF makes little sense. We can still look at the potential of future cash generation and today's valuation by checking their P/OCF ratio. At 20, it tells us that Amazon is currently trading below its historical P/OCF median. When CapEx eventually comes down, free cash flow will be much closer to OCF again, which, until then, should see significant growth.

Their gross profit is relatively stable and their P/Gross Profit ratio tells a similar story as the former multiple, as it trails below the 10-year median. It's not quite as cheap as in 2023 but still has some room for expansion.

In my view, analysts' EPS expectations seem somewhat pessimistic considering everything discussed above. A 2026 P/E of 30.7 reflects that the market knows Amazon is going to grow EPS quickly, but I still think it heavily underestimates it. Especially with earnings volatility like Amazon has it, companies often grow into and beyond their valuation much more quickly than investors can foresee, which I believe will be the case.

Risks

The primary risk to the Amazon thesis is that today’s heavy investment cycle fails to deliver returns at a pace the market expects. AMZN is spending aggressively on AI infrastructure, AWS capacity, robotics, and logistics automation, which materially lowers free cash flow in the near term. The investment case assumes that these expenditures translate into sustained revenue acceleration, cost savings, and margin expansion over time. If that conversion is slower or weaker than anticipated, valuation upside could be negated.

AWS is the most important variable. If AWS growth slows again while capital spending remains elevated, operating leverage could move in the wrong direction, pressuring margins. Similarly, while AI is expected to enhance advertising, e-commerce conversion, and logistics efficiency, the realized savings from robotics and AI-driven optimization may take longer to materialize than the market implies.

Conclusion

Amazon is a Strong Buy. It is clear that AMZN is continuing to play the long game and so should investors betting on it. A 2022 type reset can occur again, but it is important to know what one owns, which, in Amazon's case, I believe to be one of the best long-term investments in the market, from a risk-to-reward standpoint.

Rating: Initiate Strong Buy.

data.title: Best Aluminum Stocks To Leverage 2026 Demand
data.description:
Aluminum Supported by Defense, Infrastructure Spending

Aluminum prices surged above $3,000 per ton for the first time in nearly three years in the first week of January, highlighting a market increasingly shaped by tightening supply and durable long-term demand. Global inventories have steadily declined as China maintains strict caps on aluminum smelting capacity, while European output remains constrained by elevated electricity prices.

At the same time, demand from construction, renewable energy, transportation, and grid infrastructure continues to expand. Add defense to this list as geopolitical uncertainty increases in 2026, with President Trump proposing a massive increase in 2027 defense spending to $1.5T.

Aluminum Futures: 5-Year Price Movement

Aluminum prices may remain volatile, as I pointed out earlier this week with my article on top platinum ETFs, and a continuation of the macro environment supports a favorable outlook in 2026, especially for high-quality aluminum names and other metals. Against this backdrop, three aluminum stocks stand out for investors seeking exposure to both cyclical and upside in structural growth.

How I Chose the Best Aluminum Stocks

To choose the best aluminum stocks, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener, starting with the Sector and Industry filter, knowing I would target the Materials sector and the Aluminum industry, as well as Diversified Metals and Mining. I then screened for Quant Strong Buys and analyzed the five Factors – Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and Revisions – finding three aluminum stocks that fit my criteria.

1. Alcoa (AA)

  • Market Capitalization: $15.96B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/09/2025): 15 out of 278
  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/09/2026): 1 out of 5
  • Sector: Materials
  • Industry: Aluminum

Alcoa is one of the world’s largest integrated aluminum producers, with operations spanning bauxite mining, alumina refining, and primary aluminum smelting. This upstream exposure makes Alcoa highly sensitive to price movements, positioning it to benefit directly from sustained prices above $3,000 per ton. As supply tightens due to global capacity constraints, Alcoa’s cost discipline and portfolio optimization efforts could support expanding margins in 2026. The company has also focused on cash flow resilience and balance sheet flexibility, which matters in a volatile commodity environment.

Digging into Quant Factors, AA’s valuation looks attractive as its forward P/E of 17.38 gives it a slight discount to sector peers. Looking deeper, and solidifying my valuation perspective, Alcoa’s PEG of 0.31 increases its most attractive valuation metric. Supporting the growth side of its PEG, Alcoa clearly capitalized on 2025’s aluminum supply/demand combination with year-over-year EBITDA growth of nearly 95%. While 2026 may not duplicate this massive growth, it’s still robust as evidenced by AA’s forward EBITDA growth of 67%, which crushes the sector median’s expected growth of 5.29%.

For investors seeking pure-play aluminum exposure, Alcoa provides a natural entry point, setting the stage for a more specialized downstream beneficiary to spotlight next in our list.

2. Constellium (CSTM)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.79B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/09/2026): 19 out of 278
  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/09/2026): 2 out of 5
  • Sector: Materials
  • Industry: Aluminum

Constellium specializes in high-value aluminum products used in aerospace, automotive, packaging, and industrial applications. Unlike primary producers, Constellium benefits from aluminum demand growth without bearing full exposure to raw-material volatility, as it often passes input costs through to customers. In a 2026 environment defined by lightweighting trends, EV adoption, and renewable infrastructure buildouts, Constellium’s value-added product mix could support steadier earnings growth. In terms of Quant Factor Grades, CSTM’s attractive valuation metrics give it a head start among sector peers.

For an aluminum company with strong forward growth potential, its 13.4 P/E is impressive, as it offers more than a 27% discount to peers. This helps to explain CSTM’s outstanding forward PEG of 0.19, which is 85% below the sector median. Looking more closely at Constellium’s growth metrics, its 21.33% forward EPS growth crushes the sector median’s 11.15% forward earnings, while forward long-term growth (3-5Y CAGR) is even more attractive at nearly 70%, which is more than five times that of its sector peers.

Constellium’s strong positioning in aerospace and automotive aluminum makes it a compelling play on structural demand rather than pure commodity cycles. That focus on specialized manufacturing naturally transitions to our third and final featured aluminum stock, which targets niche markets.

3. Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.97B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/08/2026): 30 out of 278
  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/08/2026): 3 out of 5
  • Sector: Materials
  • Industry: Aluminum

Headquartered in Franklin, TN, Kaiser Aluminum manufactures semi-fabricated specialty aluminum products primarily for aerospace, defense, and high-specification industrial markets. These end markets offer longer-term contracts, higher margins, and reduced exposure to short-term aluminum price swings. In a tightening supply environment, Kaiser’s disciplined capital allocation and focus on premium applications may allow it to benefit from higher aluminum prices while maintaining earnings stability. From a Quant factor perspective, KALU’s strengths are in its growth, revisions, and performance momentum.

Kaiser’s growth metrics are solid to outstanding, earning it all green on its Quant factor report card. Starting with year-over-year revenue, KALU more than doubles the sector median with 7.69% growth. Looking ahead, Kaiser’s forward EPS growth is expected to be more than 25%, which is four times the sector median’s 6.38% growth. At its Q3 earnings call, Kaiser’s CEO, President & Chairman, Keith Harvey, summed up projections, stating, "Commercial aircraft recovery remained on pace throughout the third quarter with build rates strengthening and the supply chain normalization progressing, providing us with greater confidence of growing demand heading into 2026." Kaiser’s growth potential has supported positive revisions and impressive performance momentum.

As infrastructure investment and defense spending remain durable in 2026, Kaiser stands out as a lower-beta way to gain aluminum exposure. That balance between growth and defensiveness makes it a fitting lead-in to the broader investment case. For investors looking to add more than one aluminum stock to their portfolio, when combined with a primary producer like Alcoa and a value-added manufacturer like Constellium, Kaiser completes a diversified framework. This sets the stage for summarizing the macro backdrop, our thesis, and the potential risks associated with aluminum stocks in 2026.

Conclusion: Aluminum Growth and Risk Potential in 2026

With aluminum prices breaking above $3,000 per ton, declining global inventories, and structural demand from defense, construction, and renewables, the metal’s outlook in 2026 remains positive. Alcoa offers direct leverage to rising aluminum prices, Constellium provides exposure to value-added demand growth, and Kaiser Aluminum delivers specialty-focused stability. Risks to this thesis include global economic slowdown, easing energy prices in Europe, or a reversal of Chinese supply discipline. Still, for investors willing to navigate volatility, these three aluminum stocks offer differing paths to potential growth as supply constraints and long-term demand trends continue to shape the market in 2026.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like. Alternatively, if you're looking for a select number of Quant Strong Buy recommendations on a monthly basis, you might want to explore Alpha Picks.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps.

data.title: Silver: The Physical Reality Over The Paper Illusion
data.description:
Summary

It is my personal view that silver is currently in the midst of a generational bull market underpinned by structural industrial demand growth. The ongoing AI infrastructure boom only adds pressure to an already tight market and acts as a catalyst that unveils the precarious physical reality over the paper illusion of abundance. For the first time in decades, the physical reality is now in the driver's seat, and things in the silver market could escalate very quickly from here.

Silver Demand & Artificial Intelligence

We are at the dawn of a new year, and I find myself writing once again about silver. Interestingly enough, I ended 2025 by researching key players in the artificial intelligence boom, including CoreWeave (CRWV), Oracle (ORCL), and Microsoft (MSFT), among others. So what is the link between silver and artificial intelligence? What is the relationship between those two seemingly unrelated industries?

In fact, their relationship is so profound that without silver, artificial intelligence would not exist at all. Obviously, this is true not only with AI and its power-hungry data centers but with most electronic devices we all use today, ranging from laptops to smartphones and electric vehicles. Oxford Economics, in collaboration with the Silver Institute, published an interesting report on the subject last December, highlighting three structural trends that will drive industrial demand higher through 2030 and beyond, including solar energy, electric vehicles, and artificial intelligence. Silver sits at the intersection of those three industrial demand trends, and that is before talking about the monetary side of the equation.

The rise of artificial intelligence is expected to boost silver demand far beyond data centres. AI applications increasingly rely on specialised hardware such as graphics processing units (GPUs), tensor processing units (TPUs), and neural processing units (NPUs), all of which depend on high-performance semiconductors that use silver in their internal connections and packaging.

One of the key takeaways from my research on the AI infrastructure boom is how much pressure it puts on global supply chains. A great example of that would be the historic surge in demand that the North American electric grid is currently dealing with. The impact of AI data centers on the reliability of the North American grid is certainly consequential, but it is far from being the only area impacted, as similar stress started to emerge in the physical silver market last fall. As discussed in my previous article, 'The Silver Squeeze Is Here,' this is when leasing rates and borrowing fees surged to levels not seen since the Hunt Brothers' squeeze of March 1980. Following significant deliveries of physical silver to the LBMA, the worst was avoided, and lease rates went back to more normalized levels, but the core issue of physical scarcity remains.

With lease rates and borrowing costs on various silver ETFs skyrocketing, this is the type of environment in which panic buying could occur, in a bank run fashion.

The Industrial Demand Squeeze

With a raging AI infrastructure boom and the ongoing electrification of pretty much everything, industrial demand for silver is red-hot. This is what makes the current bull market fundamentally different than the previous peaks of March 1980 and April 2011. Even if the COMEX tries to increase margin requirements to keep a lid on prices, it will only work temporarily this time around, as the core buyers are of an industrial nature. They are hungry for the metal, not to speculate, but to manufacture real-world goods like solar panels, batteries, or AI GPUs. The recent off-take agreement between Samsung and Silver Storm (SVRS:CA) to restart the La Parrilla silver mine in Mexico is the perfect case in point. Yes indeed, the $7.0 million deal is small in the grand scheme of things, but I still think that it sends a strong symbolic message.

In a sense, the AI infrastructure boom is only what broke the camel's back, as industrial demand was rising rapidly well before OpenAI released ChatGPT to the public in November 2022. If AI data centers are among the most electricity-hungry devices on Earth, should we be surprised that they also have a tremendous appetite for the element with the best electric conductivity on the periodic table?

In the midst of rising geopolitical tensions between the United States and China, developments surrounding artificial intelligence have become a matter of National Security. In my view, the strategic importance of artificial intelligence may be one of the core reasons why the US Geological Survey added silver to its critical mineral list last November.

According to the 2025 World Silver Survey, industrial demand now accounts for almost 60% of global demand, up from approximately 50% a decade ago. Given the structural demand growth coming from photovoltaics, electric vehicles, and AI applications, I would not be surprised if industrial demand increases to more than 70% of global demand sometime in the 2030s. From my perspective, it is important to track industrial demand, as the metal going into this channel is ultimately consumed, not stored away in vaults, as is the case in the monetary channel.

Another potential catalyst for silver's industrial demand is the development of solid-state batteries for electric vehicles. Samsung is at the leading edge of solid-state battery technologies, as demonstrated by its 600-mile range, 9-minute charge, and 20-year lifespan, using a silver-carbon anode. Toyota is developing a similar technology, also promising a range of over 600 miles on a single charge. Even though we have not seen mass commercialization and adoption yet, this could potentially be a game-changer not only for the EV industry but also for silver demand. According to various estimates, new solid-state batteries could require up to 1 kilogram of silver for every 100 kWh vehicle battery pack.

Knowing that 17.3 million electric cars were produced in the world in 2024, silver demand could increase quite dramatically if mass adoption of solid-state batteries actually occurs. Assuming a penetration rate of 10%, we would be talking about 1.7 million solid-state batteries requiring a total of 1.7 million kilograms of silver, the equivalent of almost 560 million ounces or 50% of global demand in 2025. Mass adoption of solid-state batteries is still in the future, but needless to say, it could have a drastic impact on industrial demand as we move toward the 2030s.

Monetary Demand Adding Fuel To The Fire

In the commodity and precious metal space, silver is unique due to its dual nature as both an industrial and monetary metal. This is in contrast to copper as a pure industrial metal and gold as a pure monetary metal. Silver is exposed to both demand drivers, and in my view, the monetary side of the equation is as bright or even brighter than the industrial demand picture.

Monetary demand is notoriously difficult to track, but according to the Silver Institute, demand for silver bars and coins amounted to ~200 million ounces in 2024. Including net investment demand for exchange-traded products like the iShares Silver Trust (SLV), the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV), or the abrdn Physical Silver ETF (SIVR), monetary demand for 2024 increases to approximately 250 million ounces. Of course, nobody knows the real numbers, and those provided by the Silver Institute should only be used as rough guides.

Monetary demand for silver can be notoriously volatile, but considering the current macro environment, I expect monetary demand to continue its growth trajectory in the coming years. The U.S. national debt now stands at $38.4 trillion, an increase of more than $2.0 trillion in 2025 alone. In hindsight, we can also say that 2025 was a relatively benign year, without any significant calamity like a recession, a war, or a pandemic. When the next major storm hits the shore, how high will fiscal deficits go? At the current pace, the U.S. national debt is expected to reach $40.0 trillion sometime in 2026. How long before a crisis of confidence occurs in the Treasury market?

As discussed in my article 'The Rise Of Physical Gold And Silver Over Paper Assets' published in June 2025, geopolitical tensions are rapidly increasing all around the world. The recent events in Venezuela mark another step in the escalation of geopolitical risk. Geopolitical risks in the Middle East and in the Taiwan Strait are also increasing, and who knows how long it can continue that way before a major conflict breaks out.

Just a few days ago, mainstream media all around the world were discussing the potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz following a rapid escalation of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. There is also ample media attention around the increasing tensions on both sides of the Taiwan Strait, one of the key shipping lanes for containerships. As simulated by the Bank of England last year, a sudden crystallization of geopolitical risks could ignite a crisis in the U.S. Treasury market.

In my personal view, the next crisis is likely to be triggered by a major geopolitical event. The Federal Reserve would have to intervene once again to liquify the bond markets, and fiscal deficits would surge to new record highs in line with military expenditures. Just a few days ago, President Donald Trump actually called for the U.S. military budget to increase to $1.5 billion in fiscal 2027, the equivalent of a ~50% increase relative to the $1.0 billion approved for fiscal 2026.

At a time when the status of the Dollar as the global reserve currency is increasingly challenged, we could actually see a flight out of U.S. Treasuries during the next crisis, meaning that 10-year yields could break out above the 5.0% threshold. Actually, I think that surging gold prices are already giving a strong signal about the perceived safety of U.S. Treasuries in the current monetary and fiscal environment.

Where Will The Metal Come From?

Industrial and monetary demand for silver are both in structural growth. For strategic reasons, industrial customers are increasingly looking to secure supply at a time when investors are also looking to get their hands on the metal. Remember the old saying that if you cannot touch it, you do not own it? The higher the number of people who think that way, the higher the delivery requests on the COMEX and LBMA will go. On top of that, we already know that a significant amount of metal is moving East, as silver prices in Shanghai have been much higher than those in New York or London for quite a while already.

With all of those structural demand trends converging towards silver, the real question is, where will the metal come from? As illustrated below, primary mine production has declined from 900 million ounces in 2016 to 835 million ounces in 2025, a decline of almost 10% over the last decade or so. Because silver is primarily mined as a by-product, its supply response is largely inelastic relative to market prices.

Last summer, I researched most of the publicly listed silver miners in North America, including names like First Majestic (AG), Pan American Silver (PAAS), MAG Silver (MAG), Fresnillo (FNLPF), and Endeavour Silver (EXK), among many others. One of my key takeaways was how narrow and small the pipeline of large silver projects truly is. In my view, the restart of the Escobal Mine, owned by Pan American Silver and located in Guatemala, could be one of the largest additional sources of silver. But even then, we would be talking about 20 million ounces per year, which is a little more than a rounding error in the grand scheme of things. After almost a decade of negotiations, relations with local communities remain tense, so nothing indicates that the mine will restart anytime soon.

Conclusion

At least in the short term, an increasing amount of metal will have to come out of the above-ground stockpiles. In contrast to gold, the above-ground reserves of silver are much smaller relative to annual production. As mentioned earlier, silver is increasingly consumed as part of various industrial processes, so there is also a limit as to how much metal can come out of stockpiles. Moreover, the silver market has been in a supply deficit for several years already, meaning that a portion of the readily available metal above ground has already been exhausted.

Even if silver prices are up by more than 150% over the last 12 months, I believe we are still in the early phase of a generational bull market in silver. The rapidly increasing industrial demand is putting increasing pressure on the paper illusions, meaning that for the first time in decades, it is the physical metal that is actually driving this bull market higher.

Given the red-hot industrial demand and the massive short positions present in the paper silver market, I would expect a sustained overperformance relative to gold over the medium to long term. Now that the gold-to-silver ratio has overshot to over 100:1 in the spring of 2025, I would not be surprised if the ratio does the same to the downside. At the height of the ongoing bull market, could the gold-to-silver ratio enter single-digit territory? I think it is entirely possible and more likely than most people believe. This would make silver prices in the triple digits only a matter of time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla Adds Nvidia To Its Enemy List
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) saw a nice rally in its shares since the April lows, with the stock even making a new all-time high last month.

And yet, Tesla’s growth story continues to be challenged from all sides.

CEO Elon Musk has his fingers in many pies, but he’s not taking a big slice of any of them, which I highlighted in my last Tesla article.

In another recent piece, I also pointed out that Tesla is already losing market share in the EV space to companies like XPeng (XPEV) and BYD (BYDDY).

Meanwhile, competition is heating up in self-driving technology and robotics.

Unfortunately for Musk, Nvidia (NVDA) is now launching Alpamayo, a new “state-of-the-art” technology that promises to deliver self-driving with a novel approach.

As we move deeper into 2026, I just see evidence continuing to stack up against Tesla.

Use this as a time to get out.

Fundamentals Keep Worsening

The bad news keeps piling up for Tesla, and it’s honestly not surprising to see the stock down over 13% since its high last month.

Data for the year shows that Tesla’s China shipments fell for a second year running. Deliveries in Q4 fell 16% as EV tax credits expired.

And to round things off, EV sales are projected to grow at their slowest rate since the pandemic.

But yes, to be fair, Tesla doesn’t seem to be interested in EVs anymore, even though they're still by far their main source of revenue, and neither are investors.

We’ve been sold on the potential for AI disruption, robotics, and self-driving cars.

So what’s the latest there?

Nvidia: The Real Tesla Killer

For a long time, Tesla was ahead of the game in self-driving tech.

I can admit that, and in fact highlighted it as a reason to stay invested in the stock. But that is slowly changing.

At Nvidia’s CES 2026, the company unveiled Alpamayo and the DRIVE full-stack platform

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang framed Alpamayo as a step-change in what the vehicle brain actually does.

Today, we are introducing Alpamayo, the world’s first thinking model for autonomous driving.

Source: SA Press Release.

The key change here is the idea of “thinking.”

Nvidia is explicitly positioning autonomy as a reasoning problem rather than a pure end-to-end pattern-recognition problem.

In Nvidia’s own write-up, Huang emphasized that the model does more than convert camera/sensor input into control outputs.

Not only does it take sensor input and activates steering wheel, brakes and acceleration, it also reasons about what action it is about to take,

Source: AA.com.tr.

To make matters worse for Tesla, Alpamayo is open source, and it is only part of a much larger family of products.

Huang also highlighted growing momentum behind DRIVE Hyperion, which he described as “the open, modular, level-4-ready platform adopted by leading automakers, suppliers, and robotaxi providers worldwide.”

And this is not just a faraway pipe dream. The first passenger car featuring Alpamayo on the Nvidia DRIVE full-stack platform should hit the market soon as part of one of Mercedes-Benz’s models.

Valuation Makes No Sense Now

I’ve written about Tesla a lot, and I used to be a bull through and through.

I even laid out clear ways in which Tesla could grow into its valuation through sales of its full self-drive, or FSD, expansion into robotics, and even growth in its energy segment, which I do find quite promising.

But the reality in the last year has changed, and it would be naive for me not to also change my stance accordingly.

  • EV sales are slowing, and Tesla is losing market share

  • Tesla keeps delaying key milestones

  • FSD is being chased by other companies, including Nvidia

  • Tesla is behind Alphabet's (GOOGL) Waymo and others in the actual deployment of a self-driving car service.

As such, this valuation just doesn’t make any sense now. A P/E of 200 and a Price/Cash Flow of 100 just can’t be justified.

The issue here is that the downside is not unlimited but very high indeed. All it takes is a swift change in momentum and/or sentiment, and there's nothing stopping Tesla from dropping 50%.

It could be a slow bleed over the next five years, or it could be driven by headline news, but it will happen, in my opinion.

Upcoming Earnings

With Tesla reporting two weeks from now, on January 28th, a lot of things could change.

Consensus has revenues and earnings coming in flat. In this regard, investors are well aware of the struggles Tesla faces in the EV business.

Still, it will be important to get insights on the strategy moving forward, like price cuts, new models, and other initiatives to increase auto margins.

But what will really be important will be how Tesla frames its new ventures: AI, autonomy, and robotics.

There likely won't be much announced in terms of concrete numbers, so it will be down to the nuanced tone management takes.

More importantly, investors will be looking for reassurance that clear timeframes will be met.

A good starting point is Musk's recently approved pay package, which is tied to exact milestones.

Do these still seem realistic? And how exactly will Tesla get there?

These will be the key issues in Q4.

Final Thoughts

I still think Tesla is a great company, and they may even end up making a substantial amount of money on FSD and even robotics.

But will they dominate the market? Will they compound growth at double digits for the next decade?

This seems very unlikely while also simultaneously priced in by the market, giving investors a rather unfavorable risk/reward profile.

data.title: Expand Energy: Strong Positioning For Bullish 2026
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Hendrik Jordaan as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Expand Energy Corporation (EXE), which is a solid and inexpensive natural gas play, is the largest independent natural gas producer in the U.S. and recently further solidified its market position with a transformative merger with Southwestern Energy, another former powerhouse. Continuing domination can be attributed to its operations in Marcellus and Haynesville Shale, two of the most prolific U.S. shale plays currently.

Expand's powerful profitability demands a bullish outlook, as net income grew by $613 million (TTM from prior TTM), and it's expectation from the latest earnings release projects $600 million in merger synergies by EOY 2026, up from the original $400 million.

Most importantly, the market outlook for natural gas is strong for the coming year. Deloitte's 2026 Oil and Gas Industry Outlook projects growth "driven by rising data center demand and supportive Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) export policies." Expand finds itself in a strong strategic position to capitalize, given its market dominance and strong fundamentals.

As well, the street's bullish sentiment is correct but missing the emphasis on unit economics that fully demonstrates its potential in coming years, signaling that Expand is still undervalued by other analysts.

So, I assert a Buy rating and a long-term (by EOY 2026) price target of $125 with a 14% upside.

Introduction and Expand's Strength in a Bull Run

Expand Energy is a pure-play U.S. natural gas E&P headquartered in Oklahoma, with operations focused in Haynesville and Appalachia. It continues to scale operations, recently acquiring ~82,500 net acres of value-accretive leasehold across Western Haynesville and Southwest Appalachia in Q3. Expand's aggressive consolidation strategy, combining large-scale mergers with asset acquisitions, gives it a compounding edge that competitors cannot catch up to.

As well, more demand for O&G means top companies outperform. Their strong operating leverage, access to capital, and pricing power means Expand has a unique opportunity to outperform during the coming bull run, and continue to grow through acquisitions of land and competitors. Expand holds a solid 1,977% ($6.13 billion) in cash and equivalents to EV, more than local competitors like EQT Corporation (EQT), Coterra Energy Inc. (CTRA), and CNX Resources Corporation (CNX) with 524%, 400%, and 16%, respectively. With more cash, Expand can better leverage capital discipline and balance sheet flexibility to manage price volatility, macroeconomic shifts, and policy changes.

Financials

Assuming no extremely unusual price movements for O&G in 2026, Expand has a few financial advantages that will allow them to outperform their sector this year regardless of price. Assuming it outperforms, Expand will receive more opportunities for M&A in 2026 that will allow for even more long term value for shareholders.

With 24% CapEx to sales (TTM), 8 points higher than sector median, and CapEx to sales rising by 13% in past 5 years, Expand has been investing a lot more on itself, signaling that management cares less about short term profitability and more about long term value for shareholders. As well, updated guidance for Q4 2025 projects a reduced midpoint of full year CapEx by $75 million to $2.85 billion, as well as increased midpoint of full year production guidance by 50 MMcfe/d to 7.15 Bcfe/d. This implies Expand thinks it can produce more with less money—something that will be interesting to see in 2026. If they are right, it will further solidify their market dominance and help them grow while growing their cash stockpile to capitalize on opportunity.

Even after buying back $100 million in stock TTM, Expand's balance sheet still boasts $2.2 billion in current assets against just $2.7 billion in total current liabilities and a 2.9:1 assets to liabilities ratio. Expand recently took advantage of the declining–cost funding environment to issue $1.6 billion of long-term debt and accrued $57 million in interest from $5 billion in total long term debt in Q3.

Expand's Unique Geographic Advantage

Expand's operations in Haynesville and Appalachia give it significant exporting advantages because of its location. Being close to the East coastline and the Gulf of Mexico, Expand can use nearby infrastructure to reach international markets far cheaper than companies operating in land-locked powerhouses like Wyoming, Colorado, and Oklahoma. Compounded by significant growth forecasts in natural gas exports in 2026, Expand has the opportunity to further solidify its market position by being the inexpensive leader in the booming exports business.

Expand's location also is strategic, being in close proximity to energy demand from east coast tech companies and Virginia, the largest data center market in the world. Being home to 35% of all known hyperscale data centers worldwide, Virginia's energy demand is continually growing—projected to double by 2040. Expand's Marcellus Shale located in greater Appalachia is one of the closest natural gas reserves to Virginia, while also being extremely productive. As local demand continues to grow, Expand has a unique opportunity to leverage its current market position and strategic geographic location to generate more value for investors.

What the Market's Missing

Strong unit economics is a cornerstone in Expand's competitive edge—it ensures profitability with scale, driving more value to investors as the sector grows. Because of a drought in analysis of unit economics from other analysts, it's a strong alternative perspective that no one else is paying attention to.

Expand's gathering, processing, and transportation (GP&T) expenses have been proportionally low in 2025, and will help hedge risk against the possible expensive risks of a market downturn or sharp increase in production. Its $0.90/Mcfe GP&T expense is solid given its long-term strategy and in comparison to top local competitors. In the production powerhouses of Marcellus and Haynesville, the real risk is basis volatility driven by takeaway constraints. If production soars above pipeline capacity, local prices can plummet in a matter of weeks—which Expand has hedged against by investing in higher–capacity pipelines that are already setting them apart. As well, $0.90/Mcfe GP&T looks great versus Expand's top local competitor EQT, which has $1.03/Mcfe and less to show for it. With a lower, more cost–effective GP&T, Expand also hedges against a market downturn in ways that competitors with higher expenses cannot: GP&T doesn't lower when prices fall, companies are forced to continue paying for their expensive contracts despite making less profit.

Average realized price is another unit–based figure that looks mediocre at first glance, but will actually give significant value for investors in 2026. With $2.74/Mcfe in average realized price, Expand seemed to be selling at a discount in Q3 compared to NYMEX's $3.07/Mcfe. However, structural issues in Appalachia—experienced by all local O&G producers—are more to blame than Expand's management, who is working hard to outperform in a difficult market. In fact, trading below NYMEX presents Expand with asymmetric upside as any increase in overall Appalachian strength flows straight into realized price without any increase in capex—something that is forecasted to happen. And Expand's average realized price growth demands a bullish sentiment, as it grew 64% in Q3 compared to Q3 of 2024, showing massive operating leverage to gas price recovery despite headwinds from Appalachia.

Macroeconomic Catalysts

Supportive policies in 2026 are projected to stimulate market growth in oil and gas. According to Deloitte, "[already in 2025] executive actions expanded federal land access and eased regulations, while legislative measures also offered fiscal support through reduced royalties and bonus depreciation." They go on to estimate a continuation of these O&G-supportive policies and specifically warn oil companies to be cautious, implying natural gas might be the economically safer energy option.

The AI boom also means a boom in demand for power. According to McKinsey, global power demand is expected to increase, driven by electrification and data centers in OECD countries—a somewhat universal sentiment. As the U.S. is the leader in LNG exports, the whole energy sector should be experiencing a bull run in the coming year, but Expand has a few advantages that set them up to outperform in 2026.

U.S. LNG exports are growing rapidly, meaning American companies are able to reach more places and spread out supply across the world. In fact, the Energy Information Administration (EIA) projects North America's LNG export capacity to more than double by 2029, primarily led by increased international demand and massive investments in liquefaction. As explained previously, Expand is uniquely position to take advantage of this trend because of its strategic location—with operations extremely close to coastlines and robust exporting infrastructure.

Valuation

I used forward and TTM EV/EBITDA vs peers to value Expand because it allows for cleaner peer comparison by eliminating differences in capital structure, tax regimes, and depreciation policies—which is especially important in capital–intensive sectors like O&G.

Right now, Expand is trading at a discount with a strong 6.76x EV/EBITDA (TTM), signaling a significant mispricing in the status quo. This provides a margin of safety because the valuation is supported by current cash–making ability instead of future forecasts. As well, Expand's strong valuation matches up well vs key competitors like EQT and TPL with 8.63x and 31.15x respectively.

In the future, Expand is valued even stronger, trading at a 6.15x forward multiple. This makes Expand look even more attractive as expected EBITDA growth drives the multiple lower without assuming any increase in share price. Against peers, Expand still looks good vs EQT and TPL, which are trading at 29.07x and 8.24x respectively. I see Expand hitting $125 this year.

Risks

Commodity price volatility is a major risk for Expand, especially if the market waits for a long term period before normalization. Because Expand's revenue and returns are dependent on natural gas, oil, and NGL prices, it's exposed to a variety of risks posed by uncertainty in global supply/demand, OPEC+ actions, geopolitical events, and economic conditions. Fortunately, Expand uses an active hedging strategy to minimize this risk, using swaps, collars, and puts to lock in minimum prices for a sizable portion of its production over future quarters. However, at only 47% hedged in 2026 and minimal hedges for 2027, Expand will still face significant risks in a large energy downturn.

The Bottom Line

I estimate Expand Energy's fair value at $125 per share, and project Expand to reach it by EOY 2026. This reflects the EV/EBITDA valuation, balance sheet strength, competitive advantages, gas-cycle positioning, and advantages through unit economics.

In the short term (6-12 months), I expect relatively strong growth, although Expand's share price will be much more volatile. In line with historical precedent, I expect Expand's share price to be driven mainly by natural gas prices, winter weather, and general execution. Colder weather or a rebound in Henry Hub prices would support cash flow and share price, while weak pricing or a warm winter remain the key downside risks.

While it's notably difficult to predict energy prices, I expect Expand to outperform the sector regardless of energy demand. In a bull case, I expect revenue and net income to increase, giving more capital for M&A and capex. In a market with an energy demand downturn, I expect low to mid market cap companies to struggle and look to sell their land or companies while Expand can leverage its strong balance sheet and cash reserves to maintain high, efficient CapEx and acquire new companies, land, or leasehold.

data.title: FEGE: New Global Equity ETF With A Good Start
data.description:
FEGE Strategy

First Eagle Global Equity ETF (FEGE) is an actively managed fund launched on 12/19/2024 with an objective of long-term capital growth. FEGE has a portfolio of 89 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.17%, and a net expense ratio of 0.50%. Distributions are paid annually. As described in the prospectus by First Eagle Investments:

The investment philosophy and strategy of the Fund can be broadly characterized as a “value” approach, as it seeks a “margin of safety” in each investment purchase.

Margin of safety is defined relative to an intrinsic value calculated with an undisclosed methodology. A greater discount to intrinsic value is required for companies with specific risks (business model, balance sheet, management, regulations, geopolitics). The fund intends to invest at least 30% of its assets outside the United States, including in emerging markets. Companies of all sizes are eligible. I will use as a benchmark the MSCI All Country World Index, represented by iShares MSCI ACWI ETF (ACWI).

Portfolio

The fund has 45.5% of assets in U.S. equities. Canada comes in second position with 10.1%, followed by the U.K. (9.4%) and Japan (6.7%). Other countries are below 5%. Direct exposure to geopolitical risk related to China (accounting for Taiwan and Hong Kong) is below 4%.

The portfolio is diversified across sectors, none of them weighted more than 15%. Consumer staples come first (14.3%), almost on par with healthcare (14%), and are followed by industrials (12.3%). Compared to ACWI, FEGE mostly downplays technology and financials, while it significantly overweights consumer staples, materials, and energy.

Company-specific risk is low to moderate. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 23.8% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 3.65%. In ACWI, the top 10 weights are similar (24.3%), and the largest position is slightly heavier (4.75%).

Fundamentals

Performance

Historical data are obviously insufficient for long-term strategy assessment. Nonetheless, FEGE did an excellent start, outperforming ACWI by 13.5% annualized since 12/26/2024. Additionally, it has a shallower maximum drawdown and lower volatility, resulting in an impressive Sharpe ratio (risk-adjusted performance) in these 12.5 months.
